title: Implement Resource-Based Authorization
description: Build a resource-based authorization system where users can only edit/delete their own resources using IAuthorizationHandler, IAuthorizationRequirement, and resource-specific authorization.
instructions:
Implement 'users can only edit their own resources' authorization for ShopFlow! This is the most granular form of authorization where permissions depend on the specific resource being accessed.

**Scenario:**
You have a document management system and a product marketplace. Users should only be able to edit/delete their own documents and products they created, even if they have the 'can:edit-products' permission.

**Requirements:**

1. **IAuthorizationHandler Implementation:**
   - Create DocumentAuthorizationHandler : AuthorizationHandler<OperationAuthorizationRequirement, Document>
   - Create ProductAuthorizationHandler : AuthorizationHandler<OperationAuthorizationRequirement, Product>
   - Check if resource.OwnerId == currentUserId
   - Admins can access all resources

2. **Authorization Requirements:**
   - Use OperationAuthorizationRequirement with operations: Create, Read, Update, Delete
   - Or create custom requirements: IsOwnerRequirement, IsAdminRequirement

3. **Resource Entities:**
   - Document: Id, Title, Content, OwnerId, CreatedAt, IsPublic
   - Product: Id, Name, Price, OwnerId (seller), CreatedAt, Status

4. **Protected Endpoints:**
   - GET /api/documents/{id} - Allow if owner OR document is public
   - PUT /api/documents/{id} - Only owner can edit
   - DELETE /api/documents/{id} - Only owner can delete
   - GET /api/products/{id} - Anyone can view
   - PUT /api/products/{id} - Only owner/seller can edit
   - DELETE /api/products/{id} - Only owner can delete

5. **Imperative Authorization:**
   - Use IAuthorizationService.AuthorizeAsync(User, resource, operationName)
   - Show both operation-based and policy-based approaches

**Edge Cases:**
- Handle resources that don't exist (404 before auth check)
- Admins can bypass ownership checks
- Handle concurrent updates (optimistic locking consideration)
- Support resource sharing (future: co-owners, teams)

**Hint:** The key is the second type parameter in AuthorizationHandler<TRequirement, TResource> - this enables resource-specific authorization logic.

testCases.description: Should implement IAuthorizationHandler with resource type
Should check resource owner against current user
Should allow Admin role to bypass ownership

hints.text: Create a DocumentAuthorizationHandler that inherits from AuthorizationHandler<OperationAuthorizationRequirement, Document>.
In HandleRequirementAsync, get current user ID from context.User.FindFirstValue(ClaimTypes.NameIdentifier).
Check if resource.OwnerId == currentUserId OR user is in Admin role. If true, call context.Succeed(requirement).
In the endpoint, use await authorizationService.AuthorizeAsync(User, document, Operations.Update) to check access.

commonMistakes.mistake: Not checking if resource exists before authorization
commonMistakes.consequence: NullReferenceException when resource is null, confusing 403 vs 404 responses.
commonMistakes.correction: Load the resource first, return 404 if not found, then perform authorization check.

commonMistakes.consequence: Resource-based auth won't work - handler only gets the requirement, not the resource.
commonMistakes.correction: Use AuthorizationHandler<TRequirement, TResource> with TWO type parameters for resource-based auth.

commonMistakes.consequence: Authorization always fails because handler never signals success.
commonMistakes.correction: Call context.Succeed(requirement) when your condition is met. Don't call Fail() - just don't call Succeed().

commonMistakes.consequence: Handlers are never invoked, authorization silently fails.
commonMistakes.correction: Register all IAuthorizationHandler implementations in Program.cs with AddSingleton or AddScoped.
